result:
The moon hangs low tonight, a silver coin pressed flat against the dark thumb of the sky. I keep looking up because down here the streets are loud with lives that aren’t mine—couples arguing in two languages at once, a delivery rider threading red taillight through traffic like a sewing machine finishing its final hem before the fabric tears. I pretend the moon is a watching eye, the kind that doesn’t blink when you confess. I talk to it anyway.  

I talk about the small things first: how the barista spelled my name with two i’s, how the elevator in my building smells faintly of burnt toast and wet dog, how I still can’t throw away the postcard you sent from Reykjavík even though the ink has gone the color of weak tea. The moon listens the way stones listen—without apology, without advice. That is the kind of listening I can bear.  

When I run out of small things I graduate to the medium-sized ones: the job I quit because every morning the ceiling looked lower, the friend who stopped answering after I said depression is not a season, the plant I over-watered until its roots drowned in the dark. I tell the moon I keep replaying the moment the stem gave up and folded like a paper straw. I tell it I keep wondering whether the plant knew it was dying, whether it blamed the hand that held the watering can. The moon says nothing, which feels like forgiveness, or maybe just gravity doing its job.  

Then the big things push to the front of the line, rude and sweating. Regret in a cheap suit. Grief wearing someone else’s shoes. I tell the moon about the night my father called to say the doctors had found a new language inside his body—words made of calcium, sentences of shadow. How I booked the first flight and still arrived too late for the last coherent sentence. How I stood at the foot of the hospital bed counting the ceiling tiles because looking at his face would have made the moment real. How, when I finally looked, he was already somewhere I couldn’t follow, passport stamped in a country that doesn’t exist on any map I own.  

I tell the moon that since then I have been living in the foyer of my life, afraid to open the next door. I keep the lights on. I keep the radio tuned to talk shows where strangers argue about speed limits and cryptocurrency. Voices are feathers; they take up space without weighing anything.  

The moon drifts higher, thinning to a blade. I feel the minutes sharpening inside me. I remember that I have 1000 words, not 1000 years, and suddenly every syllable feels like currency. I want to spend them all on something that lasts longer than a breath, but everything I know how to describe is already disappearing. The heat of coffee through cardboard, the exact smell of subway brakes, the way your shoulder blade fit the hollow of my palm like a coin returned to the mint—each second after I name it, it mutates. Language is a camera that takes pictures of ghosts.  

So I try to give you the unsayable instead. The hush between two heartbeats where the universe considers reordering itself. The way loneliness tastes metallic at 3 a.m., like you’ve been sucking on the key to a room you lost years ago. The vertigo of looking at a childhood photograph and realizing the person staring back has no idea you’ll fail at half the things you promised. I want to hand you these fragments without explanations, the way a child offers a pocket full of seashells: here, these are broken, but they used to be homes.  

I want to tell you that once I walked into the ocean up to my waist and understood the word surrender is just another verb for keep going. That I turned around not because I was afraid of drowning, but because I was afraid of being the kind of alive that doesn’t hurt. Pain, after all, is evidence of proximity—like light from a star that may already be dead. We feel it anyway. We build myths about it. We call it love, ambition, morning commute.  

I want to tell you that I no longer believe in endings, only in pauses that pretend to be endings so we can sleep at night. That every story is a flashlight pointed into a cave we haven’t finished inventing. That the cave is also inside us, calcium and shadow, stalactites of memory dripdripdripping into puddles we mistake for mirrors.  

I want to tell you that I forgive you for leaving, and then I want to take the words back, fold them into a paper crane, and let it fly somewhere windier. Forgiveness is a city I haven’t moved to yet; I just visit on weekends, buy postcards, complain about the weather.  

I want to tell you that I found your old sweater at the bottom of the drawer and put it on even though the sleeves no longer reach my wrists. Growth is a quiet violence. I listened to the stitches creak. I wore it anyway because smelling like you reminded me that once I was an address worth returning to.  

I want to tell you that yesterday I saw a man on the train crocheting a scarf the color of sunrise and he was crying silently, each stitch a small knot against unraveling. No one looked up except me. I wanted to ask him whether the scarf was for someone alive or someone gone, but I didn’t want to interrupt the only thing holding him together. Instead I got off two stops early and walked home through the park where the lamps flicker like bad fluorescent thoughts. The geese were asleep, heads tucked under wings like folded letters never sent.  

I want to tell you that I still write you letters in my head while showering, steam erasing them faster than I can compose. The water gets colder and colder and still I stay, testing the temperature at which longing becomes skin, at which skin becomes just another boundary we pretend is permanent.  

I want to tell you that I am down to my final hundred words and I still haven’t said the thing that matters, but maybe the thing is unsayable, maybe it’s the space between these sentences, maybe it’s the moment you finish reading and look up—yes, that pause—where something still possible hangs, trembling, like the last leaf on a tree that hasn’t decided whether November is a ending or an invitation.  

I want to give you that leaf. Take it. Hold it to the light. See how the veins spell every name we’ve ever answered to? Press it between pages you may never open again. Years from now it will crumble at the edges, but the outline will remain, a pale map of the country we almost built together.  

I have eleven words left. I was going to use them to apologize for wasting the previous 989, but the moon is setting and the city is beginning its soft electronic hum, the sound of thousands of people simultaneously pretending tomorrow is a promise. I will spend the last eleven on a spell instead:  

May you open the door. May the cave be a cathedral. May the leaf still be green when you find it.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
